title: Pandemic
resolution_core: 4 action points per turn; discard city cards to travel and cure; infection deck escalates via epidemic reshuffles

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: All players form one disease-fighting team that wins together by discovering all four cures or loses together to outbreaks, cube shortages, or an exhausted player deck.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: Each player draws a role card with a unique rule-breaking power: the Medic clears all cubes of a color with one Treat action, the Scientist cures with 4 cards instead of 5, the Dispatcher moves other players' pawns.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.unrestricted_d: Table talk is fully open and hands are public knowledge under the rules, which makes Pandemic the textbook case of the alpha-player quarterbacking problem its descendants try to fix.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.restricted_teamma: Share Knowledge only lets you give or take the city card exactly matching the city both pawns occupy; only the Researcher may hand over any card, making card logistics a core puzzle.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur: Every turn ends by flipping infection cards equal to the current infection rate, seeding cubes onto those cities so the board pressure never stops accumulating.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.scaling_difficulty: Setup scales to player count: starting hands are 4 cards with 2 players, 3 with 3, and 2 with 4, keeping the card economy proportionate to the team size.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Players choose to shuffle in 4, 5, or 6 Epidemic cards for Introductory, Standard, or Heroic difficulty, directly tuning how often the infection deck intensifies.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.doom_track_escalat: The outbreak marker advances one space every time a city chains, and the eighth outbreak ends the game in immediate collective defeat.
vector_explanations.cards.deck_manipulation.event_deck_escal: Each Epidemic's Intensify step shuffles the infection discard pile and places it on top of the infection deck, guaranteeing that already-hot cities are drawn again soon.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.disease_outbreak: Cities hold at most 3 cubes of a color; a fourth cube triggers an outbreak that sprays cubes into every connected city and can chain-react across the network.
vector_explanations.actions.points.action_point_allowance: Every turn grants exactly 4 action points spent freely across a menu of costed actions: move, treat, build a research station, share knowledge, or discover a cure.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: A hard 7-card hand limit forces immediate discards or event-card plays whenever a draw pushes you over, punishing hoarding of cure colors.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: City cards are the currency for everything: discard the destination for a Direct Flight, your current city for a Charter Flight, a matching card to build a station, and 5 same-color cards to cure.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Deciding whether a city card is transport fuel, station funding, or a hoarded cure component is the game's central tension, since every discard shrinks the finite player deck.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: There is no individual score: the team instantly wins when the fourth cure is discovered and loses as a group the moment any defeat condition fires.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: Three independent defeat clocks run at once: the eighth outbreak, running out of cubes of any color when they must be placed, or being unable to draw two player cards.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Pawns travel a fixed network of 48 cities connected by printed lines, so route planning follows the map graph rather than a grid.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.charter_flights: Charter Flight is a named action: discard the card matching the city you are in to fly anywhere on the board, trading a curing asset for global mobility.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each turn runs a fixed three-phase sequence: spend 4 actions, draw 2 player cards (resolving Epidemics immediately), then infect cities per the infection rate track.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: Because Intensify reshuffles only the infection discard onto the deck top, skilled teams track exactly which cities can appear next and position the Medic and Quarantine ahead of the draw.

title: Pandemic Legacy: Season 1
resolution_core: Pandemic's 4-action/infection-deck engine, replayed across a 12-month campaign that permanently mutates board, decks, and rules

vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.sticker_permanen: Panic-level stickers on cities, mutation stickers on infection cards, and rules stickers pasted into the rulebook itself permanently alter every future session for this copy of the game.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.component_destru: The Legacy deck and game effects order players to destroy cards outright, removing them from the campaign forever with no way to undo the loss.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.legacy_envelopes: Numbered sealed boxes and the top-secret dossier open only when campaign triggers fire, releasing new characters, components, and rules mid-campaign.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.persistent_upgra: End-of-game upgrades let the team add permanent improvements such as positive mutation stickers on infection cards, starting research stations, and new character abilities that carry into all later months.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.progressive_rule: The Legacy deck introduces new rules month by month, with blank spaces in the rulebook filled by stickers as mechanics like quarantines, military bases, and the Faded unlock.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.event_deck_evolu: The player and infection decks physically mutate across the campaign: funded events are added or removed, infection cards gain mutation stickers, and destroyed cards never return.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.scenario_chainin: Each game's result selects the next: winning a month advances the calendar, while losing grants one retry of the same month before the story moves on regardless.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.legacy_permanent_cha: A city that riots or collapses keeps its panic level in every later game, and a disease that mutates beyond treatment stays that way, so early-campaign failures echo through December.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.replacement_characte: Characters who exceed their scar slots are lost from the campaign permanently, and codified rules govern building replacement characters from the roster and newly unlocked roles.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.campaign_arcs: The campaign runs a January-to-December arc of 12 to 24 games, with monthly objective cards and scripted Legacy-deck story beats that change the rules at arc boundaries.
vector_explanations.character.progression.permanent_scars: A character caught in a city when it outbreaks takes a permanent scar sticker imposing a lasting penalty; run out of scar slots and that character is removed from the campaign.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.legacy_map_stickers: The shared world map accumulates panic-level and Faded-city stickers that permanently change movement and infection rules for those cities, writing the campaign's history onto the board.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.campaign_progression_out: Success is measured across the whole year, not one session: monthly wins and losses steer funding, story branches, and the state of the world at the campaign's finale.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each month deals out mandatory and optional objective cards, such as curing diseases, building military bases, or quarantining cities, that replace the base game's single win condition.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.catch_up_mechanisms: R&D funding rises after every loss and falls after every win, adjusting how many funded event cards get shuffled into the player deck so a struggling team gets material help.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: The whole table wins or loses each month together and shares the same campaign fate, with no competitive scoring anywhere in the season.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: Role cards return with unique powers and now grow further apart as characters take upgrades and relationship stickers that link specific pairs of characters.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.restricted_teamma: Share Knowledge still requires both pawns in the matching city to pass that exact card, keeping hand logistics central even as the map destabilizes.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur: The infection deck still fires every turn at the printed infection rate, but Faded cities and rising panic make each flip progressively more dangerous as the year advances.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.disease_outbreak: The fourth cube still bursts a city into every neighbor, but in Legacy each outbreak also raises that city's permanent panic level, degrading it for the rest of the campaign.
vector_explanations.actions.points.action_point_allowance: Turns keep the classic budget of exactly 4 action points spent on movement, treating, building, sharing, and curing actions.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: City cards still pay for direct flights, charter flights, research stations, and 5-card cures, so the player deck remains both clock and wallet.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: Each month ends in a shared team win or loss checked against that month's objectives, feeding directly into the campaign's funding and story state.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: The classic defeat clocks of 8 outbreaks, cube shortages, and an exhausted player deck all persist, joined by month objectives that can fail outright.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Movement still follows the printed city network, though rising panic levels and Faded cities change which routes are safe or even legal to use.

title: Twilight Struggle
resolution_core: Card-driven: each card is spent for operations points or its scripted event; d6 coups and realignments under DEFCON constraints

vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.card_ops_event: Every card plays either for its operations points or its scripted event, and playing an enemy-aligned card for ops still triggers the opponent's event, creating the game's signature agonizing choices.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.defcon_status: The DEFCON track degrades with every coup in a battleground country, progressively banning coups and realignments in Europe, Asia, and the Middle East; whoever drops it to DEFCON 1 loses instantly.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.influence_placement: Operations points place influence markers into countries adjacent to your existing network, slowly building the presence that regional scoring cards convert into victory points.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.realignment_coups: Coups roll a d6 plus ops against double the target's stability to strip enemy influence, while realignment rolls contest a country with modifiers for adjacent control, both risking DEFCON collapse.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.influence_threshold_control: You control a country only while your influence exceeds your opponent's by at least the country's stability number, so control flips dynamically as markers are added and removed.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.region_scoring: Region scoring cards evaluate Europe, Asia, the Middle East, and other regions on a presence, domination, control ladder, with bonuses for battleground countries and adjacency to the enemy superpower.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Each card in hand is simultaneously ops fuel, an event, a space race discard, and a required-military-ops tool, and every use permanently forgoes the others for the round.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Sequencing when to burn dangerous enemy events, when to space useless cards, and when to hold your own bombs across 6 to 8 action rounds is the core skill of the game.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Hands are private, so inferring whether your opponent is sitting on a region scoring card drives influence placement priorities every single turn.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_passing: The China Card physically passes to the opponent after use and returns face-down, granting 4 ops (5 if all in Asia) to whoever currently holds it and 1 VP to its holder at final scoring.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: The discard pile is open and scoring cards must appear on a known schedule per era, so counting which events and scorings remain in the draw deck is essential expert play.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_reveal: The Headline phase has both players secretly select one card and reveal simultaneously, with the higher-ops event resolving first before any action rounds begin.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each of 10 turns runs a fixed sequence: improve DEFCON, deal cards, Headline phase, 6 to 8 alternating action rounds, then checks for required military operations and held scoring cards.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds: Regions score only when their scoring card is played, and holding a scoring card past the turn it must be played loses the game, making scoring timing a weapon.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: After turn 10, every region is scored at once as if its card were played, plus 1 VP for the China Card holder, resolving games that survive to the end.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: The game ends instantly on 20 VP, on causing DEFCON 1 (the phasing player loses), or on scoring Control of Europe, so many games never reach final scoring.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: The Space Race track is a public ladder: discard a card of sufficient ops and roll under the box's threshold to advance, with each milestone paying more VP to whoever arrives first.
vector_explanations.politics.warfare_politics.proxy_wars: War events like the Korean War, Arab-Israeli War, and Indo-Pakistani War resolve with a d6 modified by enemy-controlled adjacent countries, letting superpowers fight through clients without direct war.
vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.military_events: Each turn you must accumulate required military operations equal to the current DEFCON level via coups and war events or forfeit the VP difference, forcing aggression the political situation may not want.

title: Blood on the Clocktower
resolution_core: Storyteller-moderated social deduction: scripted night-order abilities, then daytime nominations and public execution votes

vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.secret_identities: Each player secretly receives a character token from the script: Townsfolk and Outsiders on the good team, Minions and a Demon on the evil team, and unmasking the Demon is the whole game.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.hidden_role_distr: The Storyteller assembles the character pool from a script like Trouble Brewing, and characters such as the Baron or Drunk secretly alter which and how many roles are actually in play.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.role_powers_night: A printed night-order sheet dictates the exact sequence in which the Storyteller wakes the Fortune Teller, Empath, Poisoner, Imp, and others to use their abilities each night.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.self_role_uncerta: The Drunk believes they are a Townsfolk and is never told otherwise, receiving plausible but broken information, so players must doubt even their own role.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.dead_player_parti: Dead players stay in the game, keep talking, keep their ability's aftermath, and retain a single ghost-vote token they may spend on one future execution vote.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.role_swapping: Demonhood itself can move: the Imp may kill itself at night to pass the demon to a Minion, and the Scarlet Woman becomes the Demon if it dies with five or more players alive.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.role_reveal_timin: Characters are never revealed on death or execution, so a dead player's claims stay unverifiable and the evil team can keep bluffing around corpses.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.moderator_or_app_r: A dedicated Storyteller runs the game from the Grimoire, secretly tracking every token, deciding drunk and poisoned outputs, and exercising judgment calls the rules explicitly delegate.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: On the first night the Minions and Demon learn each other, and the Demon additionally learns three good characters not in play to use as safe bluffs.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.accusation_endgame: With three players left alive, the day's execution is the final accusation: good must nominate and execute the Demon on the spot or evil takes the game.
vector_explanations.social.deception.misinformation_planting: Drunkenness and poisoning are formal states in which abilities malfunction and the Storyteller may deliberately feed false results, injecting sanctioned bad data into good's information network.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.bluff_claims: Everyone publicly claims characters and ability results; evil players routinely claim the Demon's not-in-play bluffs, and even good players may lie to protect key roles.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.sanctioned_lying_scop: The rules explicitly permit lying about nearly everything, including your own character, while the Storyteller may only mislead through legitimate channels like the Drunk and Poisoner.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.candidate_nomination: Executions require a formal nomination step: each living player may nominate once per day and each player may be nominated once per day, gating who can even face a vote.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.day_night_alternation: Play alternates scripted night phases of secret ability use with open daytime phases of discussion, private conversations, nominations, and a single possible execution.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.team_vs_team: Good and evil are fixed teams that win or lose together, with evil coordinating from night one and good trying to assemble trust from contradictory claims.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.elimination_victory: Evil wins by attrition when only two players remain alive, so the Demon's nightly kills are a countdown good must outrun.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Executing the Demon ends the game immediately in a good win, while executing the Saint ends it immediately for evil, making certain votes instant win-or-lose moments.

title: The Resistance: Avalon
resolution_core: Public team-approval votes plus face-down mission Success/Fail cards; zero randomness after the initial role deal

vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.secret_identities: Players are secretly dealt loyal Servants of Arthur or Minions of Mordred, and the entire game is inferring the evil minority from votes, missions, and talk.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.hidden_role_distr: The setup script is player-count dependent, and optional characters like Mordred, Oberon, and Morgana change exactly which information roles are actually present in a given game.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: The eyes-closed opening script gives layered knowledge: evil players learn each other, Merlin sees all evil except Mordred, and Percival sees Merlin and Morgana without knowing which is which.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.candidate_nomination: Each round's leader nominates a specific quest team of the printed size, so controlling nominations, and being seen as safe to nominate, is the game's political battleground.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.secret_ballots: Every player simultaneously commits an approve or reject token to each proposed team, revealed at once, so declared support and actual votes can diverge until the flip.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.face_down_commitment_: Quest members secretly play Success or Fail cards that are shuffled before reveal, so a single hidden Fail indicts everyone on the team at once.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.bluff_claims: Evil players openly claim loyalty and Morgana specifically presents herself as Merlin to Percival, while real information holders must bluff vaguely to survive the endgame.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.team_vs_team: Good and evil are fixed teams: good needs three successful quests, evil wins through three failed quests, vote deadlock, or assassinating Merlin.
vector_explanations.social.deception.assassin_card: If good completes three quests, the Assassin gets one shot at naming Merlin, converting an apparent good victory into an evil win on a correct call.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.action_trail_inferen: The vote track and quest history are public records, so who proposed whom, who approved which failed team, and who sat on clean missions become the hard evidence of the game.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.triggered_forced_r: The Lady of the Lake token, used after quests two, three, and four, forces its holder to privately inspect one player's loyalty card, injecting verified information into the fog.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Optional roles are explicit difficulty knobs: adding Mordred or Oberon handicaps good's information while Percival and Morgana add a bluffing subgame around Merlin.
vector_explanations.turns.order.rotating_start_player: The leader token passes clockwise after every proposal, so nomination power circulates and evil can simply wait for a friendly leader unless good spends rejections wisely.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each round runs the same fixed sequence: team building, public vote, then mission resolution with face-down cards, repeated across five quests.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Five consecutive rejected teams hand evil the game on the spot, and a correct Assassin strike ends everything instantly regardless of the quest score.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: Good loses through any of three independent clocks: three failed quests, the five-rejection deadlock, or Merlin's assassination after apparent victory.

title: Secret Hitler
resolution_core: Elected governments legislate from a blind 3-card policy draw: President discards one, Chancellor enacts one, everyone may lie about it

vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.secret_identities: Envelopes deal each player a secret party membership and role: liberals form the ignorant majority while fascists and Hitler form a coordinated hidden minority.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.hidden_role_distr: The fascist-to-liberal ratio shifts with player count, changing how many hidden enemies exist and how much any elected government can be trusted.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.role_reveal_timin: Roles stay hidden even in death except that killing Hitler reveals him and ends the game, and Investigate Loyalty shows a party card only to the investigating President.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: At 5 to 6 players Hitler and the fascist know each other, but at 7 or more the fascists know Hitler while Hitler must play blind, a deliberate knowledge asymmetry by table size.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.candidate_nomination: The President nominates a Chancellor under term-limit rules barring the previously elected government, so who is even eligible becomes a deduction weapon.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.secret_ballots: Every government is confirmed by simultaneous hidden Ja and Nein ballots revealed at once, letting players' actual votes contradict their table talk.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.sanctioned_lying_scop: The rules explicitly allow lying about your policy hand: a fascist President can claim to have drawn three fascist policies while holding a choice, and nobody can verify the discard.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.bluff_claims: Fascists routinely claim to be liberals forced by the draw, and post-legislation claim wars between President and Chancellor are the game's central evidence stream.
vector_explanations.politics.diplomacy.political_track: Enacted policies advance separate liberal and fascist tracks, and the fascist track's thresholds unlock escalating presidential powers such as investigation, special election, policy peek, and execution.
vector_explanations.politics.governance.laws_edicts: Legislating is procedural: draw three policies, President discards one face down, Chancellor enacts one; after five fascist policies the Chancellor may propose a veto that the President must approve.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.team_vs_team: Liberals and fascists are fixed teams with opposed win conditions, with fascists cooperating secretly while liberals must build trust from unverifiable claims.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.doom_track_escalat: The Election Tracker advances with each rejected government, and at three failures the top policy enacts automatically with no government, injecting chaos deadlocked tables cannot control.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: The deck's composition of 6 liberal and 11 fascist policies is public, so tracking enacted policies and claimed draws against the remaining distribution exposes statistical liars.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.action_trail_inferen: Voting records, who nominated whom, and which governments produced fascist policies form a public trail that liberals must read since no claim is verifiable directly.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.triggered_forced_r: The Investigate Loyalty power compels a chosen player's party membership card to be shown to the President, creating information that is itself only as trustworthy as its reporter.
vector_explanations.turns.order.rotating_start_player: The presidential placard passes clockwise each round, and a Special Election can jump it before the normal rotation resumes, making the presidency's path a strategic clock.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Electing Hitler Chancellor after three fascist policies wins instantly for fascists, and executing Hitler wins instantly for liberals, ending games regardless of track position.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: Liberals lose to either the sixth fascist policy or a Hitler chancellorship, while fascists lose to the fifth liberal policy or Hitler's execution, so both teams juggle two clocks.

title: Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective
resolution_core: Freeform lead-following: look up addresses in the London directory, read keyed casebook paragraphs, then answer quiz questions scored against Holmes

vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.mystery_structu: Each of the ten cases fixes a hidden truth and scatters redundant clues across dozens of London addresses, so multiple investigation paths can reach the same solution.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clue_chains: Witness testimony names further people and places, so investigation proceeds as a linked trail where each paragraph read suggests the next address to visit.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.newspaper_clues: Each case ships with a full issue of The Times, and buried classifieds, agony-column notices, and news items contain clues players must notice and interpret unaided.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.paragraph_book_: Visiting a lead means looking up its keyed entry in the casebook and reading the paragraph aloud, so the whole game is traversed through indexed text.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clue_deduction: Solving a case means cross-eliminating suspects and reconciling contradictory testimony gathered across leads, with no mechanical resolution system standing between players and the logic.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.london_directory_map: Players navigate 1880s London with a street map and an alphabetical directory, converting names like a tobacconist or a coroner into address codes such as 42 NW to visit.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.alibi_timeline_recon: Cases hinge on rebuilding who was where and when from fragmentary witness accounts, and catching an alibi that contradicts the established timeline often cracks the mystery.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.clue_revelation_pa: Information arrives strictly one visited lead at a time and only from the addresses players choose, so the tension curve is entirely self-inflicted by route selection.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.sealed_solution_se: The truth of each case is fixed in advance; after the quiz, players read Holmes's own solution walkthrough to see how the master traversed the same evidence.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.paid_information_a: Every lead visited beyond Holmes's own count costs 5 points at scoring, making each additional piece of information a literal purchase against your final score.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: In the standard game all players form one investigative team, debating which lead to chase next and sharing a single final score against Holmes.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.unrestricted_d: The rules impose no communication limits, so the whole table argues theories openly, and strong-voiced players can dominate lead selection.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Nothing scores until the players declare the investigation over and answer the case's questions, when points for correct answers and lead penalties are tallied in one final evaluation.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: The scoring formula subtracts 5 points for every lead followed in excess of Holmes's path, so thoroughness itself is penalized and elegance rewarded.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.graded_victory_levels: Holmes always scores exactly 100, and the players' total grades their performance in degrees from humiliation to actually beating the great detective, rather than a binary win.

title: Mysterium
resolution_core: A mute ghost passes surreal vision cards; psychics interpret them to identify suspect, location, and weapon before the clock runs out

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: Ghost and psychics are one team: either the true culprit is identified in the final vision and everyone wins, or the murder goes unsolved and everyone loses together.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: One player becomes the ghost with a screen, a solution board, and a hand of vision cards, playing by entirely different rules than the psychics interpreting the visions.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.dedicated_clue: The ghost is the sole information source and may communicate only by handing out vision cards, the tightly constrained clue channel the whole design revolves around.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.no_communicati: The ghost may never speak, gesture, or react to theories beyond an occasional knock, an absolute communication ban enforced for the entire game on that seat.
vector_explanations.social.persuasion.abstract_clues: Vision cards are surreal dreamlike paintings, so clues arrive as ambiguous imagery whose colors, objects, and moods must be mapped onto concrete suspects, places, and weapons.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.associative_clue_int: Psychics reason by association, arguing whether the rope in a vision points to the sailor or the garden well, with no logical decoding path guaranteed.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: The ghost secretly knows each psychic's assigned suspect, location, and weapon combination behind the screen, and the psychics know only what the visions imply.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.sealed_solution_se: The culprit assignments are drawn and hidden behind the ghost's screen at setup, fixing the truth before the first vision is ever dealt.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.clue_revelation_pa: Visions arrive one batch per psychic per round, and in the final round your clairvoyance level determines whether you see all three shared vision cards or fewer before voting.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.outcome_betting: Each round psychics secretly wager clairvoyance tokens on whether other psychics' guesses are right or wrong, climbing the clairvoyance track that powers the endgame.
vector_explanations.politics.elections.secret_ballots: The final culprit vote is cast face down with numbered tokens before any reveal, so psychics commit to a theory without anchoring on each other's choices.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.mulligan_redraws: Crow markers let the ghost discard any number of vision cards and redraw, a limited mulligan resource for escaping a hand that cannot express the intended clue.
vector_explanations.meta.real_time.sand_timers: After dealing visions the ghost flips the two-minute hourglass, and psychics must lock in their intuition tokens before the sand runs out.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every round follows a fixed sequence: the ghost deals visions, psychics interpret and place tokens under the timer, guesses resolve, and correct psychics advance to the next stage.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The clock board counts exactly seven rounds; any psychic who has not identified their full suspect-location-weapon trio by the seventh hour dooms the team.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: There is no individual winner: the table collectively succeeds only if the final secret vote correctly identifies the true culprit's trio.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Easy, medium, and hard setups change how many suspect, location, and weapon cards are in play, directly tuning how ambiguous each vision becomes.

title: Diplomacy
resolution_core: Simultaneous written orders adjudicated deterministically by support counting; no dice, no cards, no randomness

vector_explanations.actions.programming.simultaneous_program: All seven powers secretly write one order per unit each season, and every order on the board executes in the same simultaneous adjudication.
vector_explanations.actions.programming.hidden_orders: Orders are committed in writing and concealed until read aloud together, so every promise made during negotiation can be betrayed at the moment of reveal.
vector_explanations.actions.programming.order_conflict_resol: Hold, move, support, and convoy orders interact through formal rules: supports add strength, attacks on a supporting unit cut its support, and a dislodged convoying fleet breaks the convoy chain.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_order_ad: The adjudication procedure resolves all revealed orders at once, producing bounces, standoffs, dislodgements, and beleaguered-garrison outcomes from strict precedence rules.
vector_explanations.social.negotiation_bg.non_binding_pacts: Any deal may be proposed and none is enforced by the rules, making the stab a legal, central, and expected strategic weapon.
vector_explanations.social.negotiation_bg.alliance_promises: Play revolves around negotiated alliances, demilitarized zones, and coordinated attacks agreed in private conference, all sustained only by mutual self-interest.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.sanctioned_lying_scop: Players may lie freely about intentions, alliances, and even what they wrote; only the written orders themselves are binding truth.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.timed_communic: Negotiation happens in a designated diplomacy period before each movement phase, with the rules recommending fixed time limits after which talk stops and orders are written.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.military_comparison: Combat is pure arithmetic: one unit plus its valid supports against the defender's total, greater strength dislodges, equal strength bounces, with zero randomness anywhere.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.area_movement: Armies and fleets move between irregular provinces and sea zones whose printed borders define adjacency, coastlines splitting some provinces into distinct fleet coasts.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.forced_displacement: A dislodged unit must immediately retreat to an adjacent vacant province not contested that turn, or be disbanded, and two units retreating to the same space both die.
vector_explanations.spatial.geometry.stacking_limits: Exactly one unit may ever occupy a province, a hard stacking limit that makes every square of the map a potential standoff.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: Play cycles through Spring and Fall movement seasons followed by Winter adjustments, with supply center ownership only changing hands after Fall moves.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: Each owned supply center maintains one unit: Winter adjustments force builds in unoccupied home centers when you gain centers and disbands when you lose them.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.area_control_majority: Victory requires controlling 18 of the 34 supply centers, an absolute majority of the map's economic base, tracked openly every Winter.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.draws_and_shared_wins: The rules formally allow the surviving players to agree to end the game as a draw among all units remaining on the board, and negotiated draws are a standard tournament outcome.

title: Memoir '44
resolution_core: Command cards activate units in left/center/right board sections; custom battle dice roll unit symbols to score hits

vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Your command hand, sized by scenario to reflect each side's command quality, must be husbanded so you always hold a playable card for the section where the fighting is.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Command hands are private, so an opponent massing on your left cannot know whether you hold the section cards to answer there.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_replenishment: Every turn ends by drawing exactly one command card to replace the one played, a steady refill that makes card flow itself a pacing mechanism.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: You may only act by playing a command card: section cards order one to three units in the left, center, or right, while tactic cards like Their Finest Hour trigger special operations.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: Battle dice carry bespoke faces of two infantry, one armor, one grenade, one star, and one flag; hits land only when the symbol matches the target unit type or shows a grenade.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: The six battle-die faces are public knowledge, so players can calculate exactly how likely three dice are to hit armor versus infantry before committing an attack.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.range_brackets: Infantry roll 3, 2, or 1 dice at ranges of 1, 2, and 3 hexes while armor rolls a flat 3 dice out to range 3, so attack strength is defined by discrete range bands.
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.hex_grid: Battles play out on a 13 by 9 hex grid whose uniform adjacency governs movement, range counting, and retreat paths.
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.line_of_sight: Attacks require a traceable line of sight, blocked by woods, towns, hills, and intervening units, making terrain occupation a firing-lane decision.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.terrain_movement_modifi: Terrain hexes impose printed rules: forests and towns stop movement and reduce attacker dice, hedgerows halt armor, and rivers are impassable outside bridges.
vector_explanations.combat.morale.rout_flee: Each flag rolled forces the target back one hex toward its own board edge; blocked retreats convert into casualties, while sandbags or supported positions may ignore a flag.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.ambush_cards: The Ambush command card is played from hand out of turn when the enemy close assaults, letting the defender battle first and potentially gut the attack before it lands.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: Each scenario builds its battlefield from terrain hex overlays placed on the double-sided beach/countryside board, so the same components produce Omaha Beach, Pegasus Bridge, and dozens more.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Every scenario defines its own victory conditions, awarding medals for eliminated units and for holding marked objective hexes like bridges and villages.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends the instant one side collects the scenario's medal target of four to six medals, so tempo matters more than attrition.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Scenarios are deliberately asymmetric: sides differ in cards, units, and objectives, which is why the rules recommend a match of two games with sides swapped, totaling medals.

title: Undaunted: Normandy
resolution_core: Deck-built unit activation: play unit cards to act, bid a card for initiative, d10 attack rolls against cover-based target numbers

vector_explanations.turns.order.bid_for_turn_order: Each turn opens with both players secretly committing one card from hand as an initiative bid; the higher printed initiative chooses who acts first, and the bid card is spent.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: A unit on the board only acts when you play one of its cards from your four-card hand, so your draw literally is your command capacity this turn.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_replenishment: After acting you draw back up to four cards, and because casualties and Fog of War shrink or dilute the deck, what you refill into is never guaranteed.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Hands are private, so opponents cannot know whether you can actually activate the machine gun crew this turn or are bluffing strength with your initiative bid.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.asymmetric_starting_d: Each scenario gives the American and German players different starting decks and supplies of riflemen, scouts, snipers, mortars, and machine gunners, so the two sides play distinct tempos.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.fixed_supply_piles: The Bolster action, driven by Sergeant and Platoon Sergeant cards, adds cards from your fixed scenario supply into your discard pile, visible reinforcement options known from setup.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.deck_as_life: Casualties remove a hit unit's cards from your deck permanently, and when a unit's last card is gone the squad is eliminated from the board, so your deck is literally your roster.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.deck_cycling_tempo: Scouting new tiles adds inert Fog of War cards to your own deck, so seizing ground dilutes your draws and managing reshuffle tempo becomes a core command decision.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_rolls: Attacks resolve with a single d10 that must meet or beat a target number built from the defender's tile cover value and the range to the target.
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.cover_system: Every map tile prints a defense value representing its cover, so buildings and hedgerows raise the number attackers need while open fields leave squads exposed.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.range_brackets: Distance in tiles raises the attack target number and weapons differ in reach, with mortars able to strike distant tiles that rifle squads cannot touch.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.area_movement: Squads move tile to tile across the scenario map, and tiles must be scouted before regular squads may advance onto them, making the scouts' reconnaissance mechanical.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: Each of the twelve scenarios assembles its battlefield from square map tiles in a bespoke layout with printed cover values and objective markers.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Scenarios define specific victory terms, typically controlling marked objective tiles or eliminating enemy squads, following the 30th Infantry Division's July 1944 actions.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Attacker and defender frequently win by different means in the same scenario, one racing to seize objectives while the other wins by holding out or bleeding the assault.

title: Robinson Crusoe: Adventures on the Cursed Island
resolution_core: Action assignment with chosen risk: 2 pawns auto-succeed, 1 pawn rolls custom success/wound/adventure dice for that action color

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: The castaways win or lose as one crew: completing the scenario objective saves everyone, while a single character's death or a missed deadline sinks the whole table.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: The Carpenter, Cook, Explorer, and Soldier each bring two unique determination-powered abilities and a personal invention, so the party composition changes what the camp can attempt.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: Official solo rules have one player run a castaway supported by Friday against the same scenarios, with dedicated adjustments to difficulty and helpers.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.jointly_controlle: Friday and the dog are neutral helper pawns the team assigns collectively each round, with Friday able to roll his own risk dice and the dog restricted to hunting and exploring.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_placement: Each round the team assigns its limited pawns across Threat, Hunt, Build, Gather, Explore, Camp Arrangement, and Rest action spaces before anything resolves.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.variance_selection: Every Build, Gather, and Explore action offers the signature choice: commit two pawns for guaranteed success or one pawn and roll the three risk dice, letting the team price its own gambles.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: Each action color has its own bespoke dice trio of success, wound, and adventure faces, plus custom weather dice showing rain clouds, snow clouds, and hungry beasts.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur: An event card flips every round, dumping immediate effects plus a lingering threat onto the board before the team even chooses actions.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.delayed_effects: Event threats sit on the board with a one-or-two-round fuse: spend Threat actions to defuse them in time or eat the printed threat effect when they slide off.
vector_explanations.cards.deck_manipulation.multiple_deck_so: The game runs parallel decks: the event deck, three adventure decks keyed to Build, Gather, and Explore, the hunting deck of beasts, and mysteries, and some adventures reshuffle into the event deck to return later.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.weather_effects: The weather phase rolls scenario-specified dice: each cloud beyond your roof level costs wood and food, snow forces extra wood burn, and the beast face savages the palisade or supplies.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.settlement_managemen: The camp is a managed settlement with tracked shelter, roof, and palisade levels, and the whole camp can be relocated to a better tile via Camp Arrangement decisions.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.structural_integrity: Roof and palisade are integrity tracks eroded by storms and beasts; when weather damage exceeds them, the shortfall converts directly into lost resources and wounds for everyone in camp.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.tile_reveal: The island begins as a single beach tile; Explore actions flip new terrain tiles revealing resources, totems, natural shelters, and discovery tokens as the map grows.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.foraging_hunting: Gather actions pull food and wood from specific tile sources, while Hunt actions draw a beast card requiring weapon strength and yielding food and fur.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.perishable_goods: Unpreserved food spoils and is discarded at the end of every round, so stockpiling requires building preservation inventions rather than simple hoarding.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: In the night phase every character must eat one food or take two wounds, a mandatory ration check that turns food logistics into the island's heartbeat.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.military_comparison: Hunting is diceless comparison: weapon level meets the beast's strength, any shortfall lands as wounds, and the beast's second stat then batters the palisade.
vector_explanations.equipment.crafting.recipe_crafting: Invention cards are deterministic recipes listing required resources, terrain types, and prerequisite items, so a knife or fire is guaranteed once its inputs are secured.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each of the six scenarios sets a bespoke goal with its own timeline, such as amassing wood for the rescue pyre and lighting it when the ship passes on the Castaways scenario.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: Victory and defeat are shared absolutely: there is no individual scoring, only the scenario's collective success or failure.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: The team loses if any character's wound marker reaches the skull, and separately if the scenario's round limit expires before the objective is met.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every round marches through the fixed sequence of Event, Morale, Production, Action, Weather, and Night phases, each with its own mandatory procedures.

title: Galaxy Trucker
resolution_core: Real-time tile grabbing builds a ship from connectors, then a card-driven flight batters it with 2d6-coordinate meteor and cannon hits

vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.real_time_frenzy: Ship building has no turns at all: everyone digs through the face-down tile pool simultaneously, racing to assemble a legal ship before the timer and rivals finish.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.speed_reaction_grabs: Tiles are taken one at a time with one hand from the shared central pool, so spotting and snatching the last laser or engine before a rival is a pure speed contest.
vector_explanations.meta.real_time.sand_timers: A public sand timer paces the build: when it empties on the final space, construction stops immediately no matter how ragged your ship is.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.tile_laying_matching: Components join only where connector types match: single to single, double to double, universal to anything, and an exposed illegal join costs you the tile at inspection.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.tile_rotation_orientat: Every tile may be rotated freely before placement, and orientation is mechanical: engines must point backward and cannon barrels determine their firing direction.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.personal_map_building: Each player builds on a personal ship board whose class grid grows between rounds, and only your own layout determines how you survive the flight.
vector_explanations.simulation.technology.ship_customization: Your ship is assembled from cabin, engine, laser, battery, shield, cargo hold, and alien life-support modules, trading firepower, thrust, storage, and durability under time pressure.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.scouting: While building, you may pause to peek at the face-up adventure card stacks for the coming flight, sacrificing precious construction time for foreknowledge of the dangers.
vector_explanations.vehicles.combat.component_damage: Meteors and cannon fire destroy specific tiles at rolled coordinates, and losing a connecting piece can shear whole ship sections off into space.
vector_explanations.vehicles.combat.point_defense_countermea: Shield generators, powered by battery tokens, project protection over two ship sides and get their own step to cancel small meteors and light cannon fire before impact.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: Hit locations are rolled with two dice per row or column, so the central lanes of your ship are provably the most dangerous real estate, and good builders armor accordingly.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.charge_depletion: Battery tokens are finite charges stored in battery tiles, spent one per activation to fire double lasers, boost double engines, or raise shields, and they do not recharge mid-flight.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.cargo_shipping: Planets and abandoned ships load colored goods onto your ship that convert to credits only if you carry them to the end of the flight without losing the holds.
vector_explanations.vehicles.operation.cargo_capacity: Goods occupy specific cargo hold slots, and red hazardous goods legally require special hazard holds, so hauling capacity is a physical constraint of your build.
vector_explanations.turns.order.turn_order_by_track_position: Position on the flight track is turn order: the leading ship meets every adventure card first, claims the best planets, and overtaking reshuffles priority mid-flight.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Every component tile lost during the flight costs a credit at the round's end, so a battered ship can finish the run and still bleed away its profit.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.per_round_scoring: Credits are banked after each of the three flights for delivery contracts, finish order, and the best-looking ship bonus for fewest exposed connectors, accumulating to the final total.

title: Camel Up
resolution_core: A dice pyramid releases one camel die (1-3) at a time; stacked camels carry riders; players bet on leg and overall race outcomes

vector_explanations.luck.generators.chit_pull: The cardboard pyramid works as a randomized activation draw: shaking it releases exactly one of the remaining camel dice, and which color pops out decides which camel moves next.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: The five camel dice are custom-numbered 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, so movement distances are deliberately compressed to keep the pack bunched and stackable.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.roll_and_move: Camels advance exactly the pips shown on their released die, pure roll-and-move locomotion that players bet around rather than control.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.carrier_stack_movement: A camel landing on an occupied space climbs on top, and a moving camel carries every camel stacked above it, so one die can teleport half the pack and reorder the race.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.outcome_betting: Players win primarily by wagering: leg betting tiles pay for calling the leg's leading camel, and face-down cards from your hand back the overall race winner or loser.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.dynamic_odds_payouts: Each camel's leg-bet tiles descend 5, 3, 2, so the earliest, riskiest bettor takes the top payout while latecomers settle for scraps, and early overall bets pay 8, 5, 3 down the queue.
vector_explanations.luck.push.long_shot_gambles: Betting the overall race winner or loser early, before any leg resolves, is a codified long shot with the top payouts reserved for whoever commits while everything is still uncertain.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.probability_management: Skilled play means counting which dice remain in the pyramid this leg and betting only when the surviving combinations favor your camel.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: All five dice, their 1-to-3 faces, and which have already dropped are public, so every wager's exact odds are computable at the table.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.triggered_income: Your desert tile pays you one Egyptian pound every time any camel lands on it, an off-turn income trigger fed by other players' pyramid rolls.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: Desert tiles may only be placed on an empty track space that is not space one and not adjacent to another desert tile, with the oasis side pushing camels +1 and the mirage side -1.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds: A leg ends the moment the fifth die leaves the pyramid, immediately scoring all leg bets at 5, 3, or 2 for the leader, 1 for second, and -1 for everything else.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The instant any camel crosses the finish line the race ends mid-leg, cutting off further rolls and locking every outstanding bet.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: After the finish, the winner and loser bet stacks pay out in commitment order at 8, 5, 3, 2, 1 pounds for correct calls and -1 for wrong ones, and the richest player wins.

title: Can't Stop
resolution_core: Roll 4d6, split into two pairs, advance matching column runners; stop to bank progress or bust and lose it all

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.push_your_luck: The entire engine is push-your-luck: after every successful roll you choose to roll again for more progress or stop, and a failed roll wipes the turn.
vector_explanations.luck.push.push_your_luck_banking: Stopping voluntarily converts the white runners' positions into your own colored markers, banking progress that future busts can never take away.
vector_explanations.luck.push.bust_thresholds: The bust condition is precise: if no legal pairing of your four dice matches an active runner column, and no runner slot remains to open one, all unbanked progress is forfeited.
vector_explanations.luck.push.escalating_risk: Risk climbs as you commit: once all three runners are placed, only their three numbers can save a roll, so each additional push after full commitment is measurably more dangerous.
vector_explanations.luck.push.long_shot_gambles: The 2 and 12 columns are designed long shots: only three spaces tall to compensate for their 1-in-36 pair odds, tempting desperate players into low-probability sprints.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.variance_selection: Players tune their own variance by which columns they run: the 6-7-8 belt is a long low-variance grind while the outer columns are short high-variance dashes.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.probability_management: The core skill is steering runners toward pair-sums with the most dice combinations, exactly the odds-weighing the column geometry was built to reward.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: Four standard d6s hide nothing, and the board itself publishes the 2d6 distribution: column heights of 3 up to 13 spaces mirror each sum's true frequency.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.roll_and_keep_selection: Each roll of four dice is partitioned by the player into two pairs, choosing which sums to keep and which to waste, converting raw randomness into a constrained allocation choice.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends the instant a player claims their third column, making speed of ascent the only score that exists.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.area_denial_blocking: A claimed column is dead for everyone: its number no longer counts for any player's rolls and rivals' progress markers on it are removed, so claiming 7 amputates the richest number from the game.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Columns are visible first-come-first-served prizes: whoever tops out a column claims it permanently, and three such claims end the game in victory.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.variable_turn_length: Turn length is entirely self-determined: a cautious player stops after two rolls while a greedy one strings a dozen, so the effective size of a turn is itself the central decision.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Play passes strictly clockwise with each player completing an entire press-or-stop sequence before the dice move on.
